cards.text: To pass a std::array to a function without copying it, use {{c1::pass by (const) reference}}: void func({{c2::const std::array<int, 5>&}} arr)

cards.front: What happens when you pass a std::array by value to a function?
cards.back: The entire array is copied element-by-element, which can be expensive for large arrays. This is different from C-style arrays which decay to pointers.

cards.question: What's the issue with this function signature?
cards.answer: Missing the array length template parameter. std::array requires both type and length.
cards.explanation: std::array needs both template parameters: std::array<int, 5>& arr. The length is part of the type.

cards.text: To write a function that accepts std::array of any length, use a {{c1::template function}} with a {{c2::non-type template parameter}} for the length

A template function accepting any std::array of ints: {{c1::template <std::size_t N>}}
void func({{c2::const std::array<int, N>&}} arr)

cards.front: Can you return a std::array by value from a function?
cards.back: Yes, you can safely return std::array by value. The compiler will typically optimize it using RVO (Return Value Optimization) to avoid copying.

cards.text: When returning a std::array from a function, you can use {{c1::auto}} as the return type and the compiler will deduce it

cards.concept: Best practices for passing std::array to functions
cards.explanation:
Prefer pass by const reference for read-only access:
  • Avoids expensive copying
  • const prevents modification
  • Works for any array size if using templates
Use pass by reference (non-const) when you need to modify the array.

cards.front: Can a function accepting std::array<int, 5> also accept std::array<int, 10>?
cards.back: No, they are different types. std::array<int, 5> and std::array<int, 10> are completely distinct types. You need template functions or overloading to handle different sizes.

cards.text: In C++20, you can use abbreviated function templates: void func({{c1::const auto&}} arr) to accept any std::array

cards.question: What does this function return?
cards.explanation: Using CTAD (Class Template Argument Deduction) in C++17+, the compiler deduces the type as std::array<int, 5> from the initializer list.

cards.front: Why is returning std::array by value generally not a performance concern?
cards.back: Compilers use Return Value Optimization (RVO) to construct the return value directly in the caller's location, avoiding the copy. Additionally, move semantics can be used if copying is necessary.
